title: Maybe You Should Talk to Someone

chapters.title: Vulnerability sparks magic
chapters.text:
Life has a habit of tossing curveballs at the worst possible moment. When a rough patch lands, how do you respond? A common move is to put on a brave face and throw up walls. That move is normal, but it rarely serves you well over time.

There is real strength in sharing what you feel.

Working with a counselor gives you a trained partner for the journey. Counselors spend years learning how to walk folks through the storms they face.

In her Los Angeles office, Lori Gottlieb listens to her patients, offers comfort, and walks them through their struggles. Still, Gottlieb cannot steer every part of her own life. So she sees a counselor as well.

Beyond reaching out during hard moments, mental health pros sit in the patient chair as part of their training. It helps them handle the emotional weight of the job. Talking through their own storms with fellow clinicians guards against burnout and keeps their minds healthy.

Sitting in the patient chair also lets counselors see the process from the other side.

Gottlieb shares insights from her dual role as a clinician and a patient. She breaks the myth that counselors are immune to hardship, glide through life, and function without any support.

So get ready to see how Lori Gottlieb handles her own issues and those of her patients. Let this summary show you that asking for help is nothing to be ashamed of.

chapters.title: Counselors may learn from their patients
chapters.text:
Lori Gottlieb has sat with many clients, including John, a self-absorbed Hollywood writer and producer. The man, who she thought was a piece of work, said he was stressed, could not sleep, and struggled to get along with his wife. John called anyone he could not figure out an idiot, from his coworker to his dental hygienist to his last counselor, who only lasted three sessions. Stuck in a cycle of blame, he never looked at his own role in the issues he faced.

Lori tried to steer the talk in a more useful direction, but John took over the conversation. To understand him, she searched for any positive traits. He was a hard shell to crack, yet Gottlieb believes that getting to know anyone deeply always uncovers something to like. She once even found a sweet side in a person who had tried to commit murder.

Every person has a story and a unique view to share.

While guiding John, something struck Lori out of the blue. Maybe they were not that different after all. Gottlieb was dealing with her own storm. Her boyfriend, the man she thought she would marry, had ended things the day before. In her pain she had called him an idiot and pinned all the blame on him for the sudden split. Just like John, she had been stuck blaming her ex without looking at her own part in the breakup.

We are reflecting mirrors, showing each other what we cannot yet see, as Lori Gottlieb has written.

This realization pushed her to look at her own behavior and accept that she needed someone to listen and offer an outside view. Beyond that, the weight of hearing painful, delicate stories as a counselor was wearing her down. So Gottlieb decided to reach out and start seeing Dr. Wendell, another counselor a mile away.

You can find clarity by exploring your thoughts and feelings.

Active listening, empathy, and acceptance are key parts of effective counseling.

chapters.title: Whatever bothers you might be just a symptom
chapters.text:
In counseling, the presenting problem is the issue a client brings to the first session, the reason they asked for help. Often, that issue is just the tip of the iceberg. As sessions continue, deeper problems tend to surface. Working only on the surface issue gives short-term relief. Real healing happens when the hidden issues come to light.

When Gottlieb went back to counseling, her presenting problem was the sudden breakup with her ex-boyfriend. She called him The Boyfriend.

The Boyfriend, who Gottlieb described as the kindest man she had ever met, ended the relationship out of nowhere after two years together.

He was the type who would drive to the pharmacy at 2 a.m. for her antibiotics without a word of complaint. He left sweet post-it notes on her desk, held her hand, opened doors, and never grumbled about being pulled to her family events. He sent her Amazon packages full of books, since he knew books were her love language, for no reason at all, and he seemed to adore her family.

And then he dropped the bomb. He did not want to spend the next ten years with Gottliebs child under his roof. The Boyfriend admitted that he had never planned to date a woman with kids, but he had fallen for her and lost his footing.

When Lori sat down with Dr. Wendell, she expected him to agree with everything she said about The Boyfriend. As the sessions moved on, it became clear that she was not just grieving the end of a relationship. A deeper fear hid beneath the sadness, a fear of death. Gottlieb was facing the unsettling truth that she was getting older, and that stirred up anxiety about her chances of finding love again. This split hurt more than the others because it touched an existential fear.

It is okay if you are going through a hard time but cannot name the deeper issue. To uncover the hidden source of your struggles, try setting aside a few minutes each day to journal. You can free write whatever comes to mind, or you can focus on specific topics you want to explore.

When you journal, try not to judge your feelings. Simply notice them with curiosity.

chapters.title: Instead of running from your feelings, look them in the eye
chapters.text:
One of the core ideas in counseling is to explore emotional blocks. A patient resistance to a topic can point straight to the root cause of their issues. Anxiety and discomfort around a subject may hint at an old trauma that is still shaping daily life.

Remember John, the one who called everyone idiots? One day, a name slipped out during his rant, Gabe. It turned out that John had a son, but in all their earlier sessions he had worked hard not to mention him. When Gottlieb tried to dig into the mention, John pushed back hard. He became guarded and scared and even briefly decided to quit counseling. Yet Lori sensed that the missing piece he was avoiding was the key to helping him.

After some time, John steadied himself. He shared the truth. Gabe was indeed his son. A terrible car accident had taken him from John and his family. It was not the first loss he had faced. Johns mother had died when he was young. Those tragedies were the hidden source of Johns anger and frustration. Only by facing the grief, letting himself feel the pain, and working through it could John start to heal. He and his wife began to accept their loss and learn to live with the grief rather than bury it under anger. That hard work helped them become better parents to their daughters, the children who had survived the crash.

You can use the idea of resistance in your own life to better understand your feelings and actions. When you bump into a topic or situation that triggers you, your first instinct may be to avoid the feeling. Yet by turning away, you might miss the very insight that helps you heal.

Your pain is your compass, not your anchor.

No emotion is bad by nature. Each one is a messenger with something to say. Your job is to figure out what.

To move forward, you have to face your emotions head on. The process can be tough and at times painful, but it can also change you. Your self-reflection can begin with a journal, a call to someone you trust, or a session with a professional.

chapters.title: Depression is too piercing to ignore
chapters.text:
Time to meet another character who will help us decode some of life lessons. Rita was one of Lori Gottliebs patients, a divorced woman whose presenting problem was depression. She saw her life as a long string of mistakes. She believed she had drifted far from her true path and lost sight of the mother and person she wanted to be. Rita had given herself a year. She had grim plans to give up the fight if her life did not change.

When Rita walked into her first session, her depression was so deep it felt like she was reading her own eulogy. In her eyes, her life story was a tragedy, a finished one at that. With three failed marriages behind her, four grown and troubled children, no grandchildren, a quiet apartment, and a job she had never enjoyed, Rita saw no reason to get out of bed each morning.

She carried a long list of regrets. She thought she had married the wrong men, put her needs ahead of her children, never turned her artistic talent into a paid career, and never built a supportive community when she was young. Given all of that, it did not surprise her that her children resented her. She had hidden behind denial for a long time, but recently that defense had stopped working. Even her love of painting, the only thing that once gave her joy and a sense of achievement, barely held her attention anymore.

As Ritas seventieth birthday drew near, she had made a private deal with herself. She would either turn her life around or consider ending it. However, Lori Gottlieb sensed that Rita wanted to change, not to die.

In every situation, there is room for happiness and meaning.

Ritas journey shows how regret can take over a life if it is left unchecked. Her self-criticism and negative self-talk convinced her that her life was a failure with no hope. Through counseling, she came to see that her life was hers to change. Even with strong resistance, Rita began taking small steps toward a fuller life, even when it meant stepping out of her comfort zone.

chapters.title: Past mistakes are lessons, not losses
chapters.text:
In an emergency session, Rita was on edge, far from her usual put-together self. A man named Myron, once a pal from the mailbox, had been in her life for a stretch. Back then, he was her only friend, and he invited her to a Saturday market run. Rita assumed it was a romantic ask, so she put on a show of saying no. Yet they drifted into a warm friendship over time. They shared the same hobbies, and although they were not an item, they were rarely apart. Rita had written Myron off as passable looking, but something shifted in her one day.

She felt she had to speak up about where their friendship was headed. But she froze, and the moment slipped past. By then Myron had already started seeing Randie, a much younger woman he had matched with online. Rita was gutted and quietly set a deadline for herself, end things at seventy if life stayed this gray.

Three months on, Myron and Randie split. When he told Rita he wanted to grow old with her, she made a call to lay her past bare so he could choose with open eyes.

Love pays no mind to age or social circles. Anyone can fall in love.

Rita penned a letter to Myron about a lonely childhood, a long fight with depression, and how it had shaped her children. Reading it aloud to Gottlieb through tears, she also brought up her first husband, who had been abusive. She admitted that her own neglect, stacked on top of his drinking, had wounded her children and left them distant.

Ritas arc proves you are never too late to take the wheel. Change can feel like stepping off a cliff, and the unknown always carries a chill. Still, a fuller life waits on the far side of that fear.

On top of that, this gutsy woman worked hard to make peace with herself. Rita found the nerve to walk her estranged children through everything, and she put real effort into building something real with Myron.

Self-forgiveness is not a selfish move. It can actually be the bridge back to making things right.

chapters.title: Denying a problem will not make it go away
chapters.text:
Lori Gottliebs newest client, Charlotte, said she enjoyed a couple of glasses of wine each night to relax. That turned out to be the edited version. When Charlotte came in for anxiety and depression, she was in denial about her drinking. She drank more than a few glasses and started having gaps in her memory. She did not think anything was wrong with social drinking, and she worried that her real addiction was her counselor, Lori Gottlieb.

Charlotte hated needing Gottlieb as much as she had needed her parents. She dated unavailable men because she was drawn, without realizing it, to partners who copied her fathers emotional distance from when she was young. Her parents had been absent much of the time while raising her. They often fought, cursing and yelling so loudly that the neighbors sometimes complained. They were never attentive, and because of those early experiences, Charlotte had linked love to a feeling of anxiety rather than calm or warmth.

Charlotte used alcohol as a crutch to manage her anxiety and depression. Relying too heavily on a habit keeps a person from dealing with the root cause of their problems and from learning healthier ways to cope.

One of the biggest lessons from her story is the long shadow of childhood. Charlottes early years left a lasting mark on how she saw love. Even though she wanted to escape toxic relationships, fear kept her from reaching for healthy ones.

We cannot have change without loss, which is why so many people say they want change but stay exactly the same, as Lori Gottlieb has written.

If you are dealing with similar struggles, remember that your past traumas are not your fault and that you are not alone.

Your childhood shapes your life, but it does not define you.

Denial can be a dangerous thing. Since Charlotte refused to admit her addiction, her drinking got worse. It was only when she entered treatment that she was able to face her unhealthy behavior and start to recover. Her case shows the value of being honest with yourself and naming problems, even when it is uncomfortable.

In addition, working through her childhood traumas allowed Charlotte to face her fears and insecurities. That hard work gave her the power to rewrite her story.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Across his sessions, John slowly picked up more empathy and learned to talk things through with his wife. They both knew the path to healing was long and slow, yet they were willing to walk it side by side.

Rita stepped onto the road to forgiveness and healing, finally breaking out of the cage she had built around herself. The sessions did their job. She just needed someone to listen. Myron took in Ritas letter, sat with it for a while, and decided to bet on her. Rita also fell back in love with art and started teaching it at a local college. She even set up a small website to sell her paintings.

Charlotte walked into Alcoholics Anonymous and stuck with the program. She poured the wine out for good and cut ties with the old crowd that cheered her drinking on. She also let a healthy, warm, and empowering kind of love into her life.

Like John, you can dig up the hidden feelings that quietly drive your days.

Like Rita, you can hand yourself a little grace and reshape what your life is about.

Like Charlotte, you can get clear on your struggles and step onto a brighter path.

Like Lori herself, you can keep working toward inner peace while pouring a little light into the lives around you.

If you need to talk to someone, look up the nearest counselor and book a session. If the price tag is too high, there are other doors to knock on. Community centers, faith groups, and local charities often run low-cost or free support. University clinics pair grad students in psychology or counseling with clients under the watch of licensed pros. Online counseling and counseling apps are also worth a look. And a quick call to a helpline can put you in touch with someone who will listen.
